articles.title: The Armed Forces Find Woman Has a Place
articles.author: Nona Brown
articles.description: Nona Brown discusses the allowance of women to serve as permanent members of the armed forces.
articles.intro: On June 12, 1948, women were permitted to serve as permanent, regular members of the armed forces with the passing of the Women's Armed Services Integration Act. In this article from The New York Times, Nona Brown discusses the new female recruits and their training.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the reasons given for integrating women into the armed forces.
articles.permissions: From The New York Times , December 26, 1948 © 1948 The New York Times. All rights reserved. Used by permission and protected by the Copyright Laws of the United States. The printing, copying, redistribution, or retransmission of this Content without express written permission is prohibited.

blocks.text: When 412 young military recruits started hupping and saluting in three new trainings this fall, something new was definitely added to the American way of life. These recruits were women — girls, really — the first in the nation’s history to sign up for three-year hitches in the regular military services.

There was little, however, in the scenes at the Great Lakes Naval Training Station, near Chicago, or in the Army’s Camp Lee, in Virginia, or at the sprawling Lackland Air Force Base, near San Antonio, Tex., to show that this was a major milestone in the relentless feminine march toward equal pay, equal rights, and equal responsibilities with men.

To the women involved — pioneers of the full peacetime quota of 18,000 military women to be enlisted by June, 1950 — the historical significance of their act was far less important than the varied career opportunities which the armed forces offer. They are facing a dazzling display of training and job opportunities. Their field of choice includes work in radio and radar, transportation routing, control tower and flight orderly duties, photographic, hospital, legal and many other specialized skills.

In fact, the Army, Navy and Air Force are willing to let these eager new recruits try their hands at just about any job the women think they can do. The object is to find out, under fairly quiet peacetime conditions, just what types of military work women can do best.

Top military brass had to present very cogent reasons to the Eightieth Congress in order to persuade the somewhat reluctant male legislators that women not only can but must have a place in contemporary military organizations.

They pointed out that the closer the world comes to push-button warfare, the closer comes the day when women in Pittsburgh or Oklahoma City may find themselves on a battlefield. From the standpoint of safety, home and the front lines would be almost indistinguishable. Furthermore, in any future war the United States must be able to use every last ounce of human strength. Obviously, this means womanpower as well as manpower.

It would be risky, continued the military leaders, to wait for another war emergency to bring women back into service. That would be as costly and timewasting as it would be to abolish the Army itself on the assumption that it is really civilians in uniform who fight and win our wars. Although reserves form the bulk of our fighting forces in wartime, they are only as effective as the direction given them by men who have made military matters their life business.

By the same token, the military spokesmen concluded, civilian women can best be used by the armed forces in wartime if some women have already had experience in the military jobs women can fill. At the same time, because of their special aptitudes, these women regulars will be making a direct contribution to the efficiency of many military operations.
Congress finally accepted this argument and passed the Women’s Armed Services Integration Act of 1948. Today’s new recruits are heirs to the job bonanza it implies.

They don’t mind in the least being looked upon as laboratory specimens, or “a nucleus appropriately adaptable to expansion,” as the Army expresses it in a general directive. For therein lies their wide variety of job opportunities.

The Navy, for example, feels it will be worthwhile to try training women in thirty-one of the sixty-one enlisted ratings. (Almost all of the forbidden thirty are construction or shipboard ratings, such as steelworker, boilerman, gunner’s mate.) The Army expects to distribute its women, in small detachments, to these departments: medical, ordnance, chemical warfare, finance, transportation, signal and adjutant general. The Air Force will do likewise.

The chance to test and experiment in fields of work new for women will, of course, be extended to Wac, Wave and Waf officers, too. They will serve as doctors, lawyers, dietitians, meteorologists, chemists, physicists, supply and finance officers, medical specialists, public information and personnel officers, and so on down the list of virtually every shore or non-combat officer job. Graduate training in many of these specialties will be as available to qualified women as to qualified men.

Only a minimum of women officers and non-coms will be involved in the training or supervision of servicewomen. The Air Force and Navy plan a co-ed system of training and duty assignments (except for recruit schools), thus eliminating the need for separate women’s staffs. The Wac may need a few more staff members because, although it will use joint training schools, it will retain its separate corps structure.

One corollary of the military’s experimental outlook for women that the women themselves like most is the policy decision that only a limited number can be assigned to clerical work. The Navy, for example, has imposed a ceiling: not more than 20 to 25 percent of its enlisted women will do office work. Army thinking on this matter is about the same as the Navy’s.

For the Air Force, Col. Geraldine P. May, Director of the WAF, points out, “After all, it is no secret that women are excellent office workers. Why should we waste the 4,000 enlisted women the Air Force can have in proving what we already know.”

The only exception to this attitude comes from the Marine Corps, where the essentially combat nature of the entire corps makes it almost impossible to assign women to anything but clerical jobs. As a result, the 800 or so women to be enlisted in the regular United States Marine Corps will be limited specifically to office duty in the Washington or San Francisco areas.
“Free a Marine from a desk job, to be ready to fight, is our peacetime motto,” says Col. Katharine A. Towle, newly commissioned Director of Women Marines.

In the three main services, uniformed women will work with, rather than replace men. They will have a chance to try for any kind of billet from weather observation to electronics. In the long run, this will give the services a clear, documented answer to the question of where women fit best in the military, where they can be used most quickly and contribute most, in the event of total war.

It goes without saying that this willingness to experiment is based on what the reserve Wacs, Waves, and Wafs did under the necessarily haphazard assignment system evolved in the frantic emergency conditions of the last war. Then, the services quickly found that women were actually better than most men, not only at office work but in many hospital jobs, in cryptanalytic and communications work, in postal and supply billets, and in personnel jobs. Further, they found that women’s manual dexterity was valuable not only on a typewriter keyboard, but in maintaining and repairing the multitude of intricate small instruments essential to mechanized warfare.
The reasons why women proved so skillful in these disparate fields were several.

First of all, women don’t mind routine jobs; they will be patient, attentive and quick in carrying out the most boring, tedious of tasks. Communications work, for example, is considered the ne plus ultra in monotony, but the Waves didn’t seem to mind. In fact, they constituted three-quarters of all the people working during the war at the Navy’s huge Washington communications center.
Women, too, are keen with their eyes and quick with their fingers. Thus, the Army used many in photo interpretation work and the Navy used many in the repair of small aircraft instruments.

Another feminine aptitude which the military found valuable is women’s patience with other people. In civilian life, this has long been recognized in the predominance of women as teachers and nurses. In the military, they made excellent Link Trainer and aerial gunnery instructors; they were used by the thousands in medical services.

Military commanders also discovered that women were extremely loyal and conscientious — so much so that instances where women worked, voluntarily fourteen to eighteen hours a day were not rare. Whatever their vigil, whether in an air station control tower or a hospital experimental laboratory, their attentiveness was complete. Thus it is that today there is one pilot at Lackland Air Force Base in Texas who would be happy to see women in almost any non-combat job, for this lieutenant owes his life to the stick-to-it-iveness of a Wac radio direction finder crew in France.

One tentative debit has showed up on the distaff side of the military ledger: it is not economical to assign women to jobs requiring real physical strength. For example, the Army, Air Force and Marine Corps use a number of women as truck drivers during the war but will do so no longer. A woman can, if necessary, drive a two-and-a-half-ton-truck, but she can’t unload it.
The Navy, likewise, doesn’t plan at the moment to use women as aviation ordnancemen, a rating held by a few wartime Waves. The reason is that the job involves lifting or moving heavy weights.

All this experience, all these generalizations on women’s military aptitudes and skills are already undergoing thorough analysis by Army, Navy and Air Force personnel specialists. And, of course, the performance of the women regulars under the more exacting standards of direct peacetime competition with men will be carefully weighed in the ultimate conclusions. But at this moment, the personnel analysts are willing to bet that the peacetime Wacs, Waves and Wafs will show themselves capable of handling almost every job their wartime predecessors did, and perhaps a good many more.

The question arises as to whether the women who choose to sign up for a military career are the kind who can fulfill all these hopes and expectations. If the group of Air Force recruits at Lackland is at all typical (and there is every reason to suspect that it is), then the armed forces should have no trouble on this score.

At first glance, of course, there was nothing about these Waf “basics” to distinguish them from the 300,000 women who had gone through the military mill during the war. A masculine eye probably would have noted that the new recruits were generally good looking and pleasantly young. But if the cogs were more attractive, the military machine into which they were being fitted rolled on in the same old groove.

These first eighty-four new Waf recruits even found themselves housed in the old Wac barracks area, repainted, to be sure, and with a new Non-Com’s Club added. They went through the same mile-a-minute processing. They came out of the line-up still rubbing their arms where those long needles had gone in, already garbed in varied, ill-fitting uniforms that time and tailors will fix. They were self-consciously obedient to the snappy commands of their feminine training officers and non-coms. And still typical of military women, they marched so well that old timers commented that the “basics” put them to shame.

Then, at the same smooth speed, the newcomers began the training routine, marching in “flights” to and from classrooms, marching to and from the mess hall, and forming, each sundown, for retreat. Their training curriculum, however, is fuller now than the hurry-up wartime schedule: the entire course will take eleven weeks instead of eight. They will study the same subjects as men recruits except for small arms training.

The young girls were cheerful about their new mode of living. “It’s really kind of fun having so many other girls around, and from different parts of the country, too. I’m learning a lot.” Or, “When I have to scrub the floor, I begin to wonder what I’ve let myself in for, but then I figure, I asked for it.”

The outstanding impression gained from the Waf recruits is that they are eager, intelligent persons who have made a serious, long-range decision. They have embarked on careers which many of them expect to stick with for twenty or thirty years, until retirement age. Once in a while, you’ll hear a flip remark about being a “thirty-year Waf or permanent wife,” but otherwise the presence of thousands of men at Lackland seemed to make little impression on the women.

They are also remarkably unimpressed by the fact that men should consider them capable of doing “men’s jobs.” If a questioner asks them why they signed up, the answers come back without any missionary or feminist overtones. A group of them, relaxing after lunch in the mess hall, recently provided these views on the matter:
“I decided to join up,” remarked an 18-year-old, “because I need more of an education and my family couldn’t afford to give it to me.”

“That’s part of my reason too,” said a young blonde from the Middle West. “I’ve had three years of college but there wasn’t enough money to finish. The inflation bogey was really what made up my mind for me. What with high prices and the possibility of another war, I just couldn’t see any way to get job security or plan for the future. The Air Force offers me both.”

A freckled redhead from Hollywood snuffed out her cigarette and began speaking. “With me, it was inflation and getting training for a good career. And then, I’ve got another reason you probably won’t believe. My grandmother has what you might call a crazy idea — that every member of the family owes some service to her Government and her country. We’ve all gone along with her, either in civil service or the military. This seemed to me a wonderful way to discharge my obligation.”
“I joined to get away from the Seventh Avenue Subway — and a steady date with a filing cabinet,” was a Bronx girl’s forthright explanation.

The eyes of a Southern girl who had just come of voting age were completely serious: “I’m an Army brat. As far as I’m concerned, I’m in for twenty years, readying to do anything the Air Force thinks I can.”

Listening to the group, a number of descriptive adjectives came to mind — poised, good-humored, adaptable, clean-cut. Most of the recruits are these things and more. All of them, as well as their sisters in the other services, are high school graduates. Many have had some college education. And all have been holding responsible civilian jobs, even if unimaginative or clerical in nature.

The conclusion is inevitable that the services in peacetime have attracted a much higher caliber of enlisted woman than the average wartime enlistee. To be sure, these first recruits have been most carefully selected and there is some question as to whether this high standard can be maintained, but the services are optimistic. If the optimism is justified until the quota is filled, then the armed forces should have no trouble in carrying out all the experiments they want to in the uses of women in military assignments.

The four heads of the permanent women’s services, Col. Mary Hallaren of the Army, Capt. Joy Hancock of the Navy, Colonel May and Colonel Towle, are under no illusion, however, that just because there seems to be no personnel problem the road ahead will be completely smooth. There are other troublesome factors — several of them.

One is the problem of morals: of convincing mothers, fathers, brothers, Congressmen, service men and junior officers that women really can be military without being camp followers or without being converted into rough, tough gals who can cuss out the chow as well as any dogface. Another is to make sure that service women actually are trained and assigned in as wide a variety of jobs as possible. All too clearly do the policymakers, both men and women, know that the mass of military men cling strongly to the idea that if women won’t stay in the kitchen, they should surely go no further than the typewriter and filing cabinet. The third problem is the one of getting men and women to work well together, on equal terms, on the same project.

To some extent, the first two problems can be met through written directives on the selection, training and assignment of women. But the solution of the third depends upon the education of the public and the uniformed male animal. It is no secret that men, traditionally lords and masters of the better jobs, are both resentful and skeptical of the whole idea of distaff equality. It was amusing, at Lackland, to discover how many men thought it was a fine scheme to use women in the Air Force — in somebody else’s outfit!

In view of this, women in the Army, Navy, Air Force and Marine Corps will do well to heed the words of the sergeant in charge of the radio shop at a large station. After reluctantly admitting that a smart woman might possibly be taught to repair radios, he allowed he might even be willing to have one assigned to his shop, “if she’ll keep her mouth shut!”

options.label: Young women should be encouraged to enlist in the military now so that they may serve on the front lines of combat in future total war.
While women were permitted to enlist in the military, most women voiced a desire to remain in noncombat roles that were not labor-intensive.
Although there are some concerns about women enlisting in the military, leaders are hopeful that making this transition during peacetime will better prepare the military.

While womanpower is needed in the military, many men fear that this will lead to a decrease in military readiness and increase in conflict in the ranks. E. Despite being permitted to enlist in the military, the jobs women could take are unjustly limited due to persisting gender stereotypes and resistance from leaders. F. Many women are eager to join the military to pursue their own personal goals through the variety of opportunities available.

articles.title: Eleonora
articles.author: Edgar Allan Poe
articles.description: A man poetically recounts his tale of childhood love—and loss—with a somewhat happy ending.
articles.intro: Edgar Allan Poe (1809-1849) was an American poet and author who wrote during the Romantic period—a literary movement characterized by intense emotions, glorification of the past, and strong individualism. Poe is known for his macabre tales of horror and death that give insight into the human condition. This story is considered to be semi-autobiographical.
articles.annotationTask: As you read the story, take notes on Poe’s use of imagery and symbolism, and the way he depicts youth, love, and loss.
articles.permissions: Eleonora by Edgar Allan Poe is in the public domain.

blocks.text: I am come of a race noted for vigor of fancy and ardor of passion. Men have called me mad; but the question is not yet settled, whether madness is or is not the loftiest intelligence— whether much that is glorious— whether all that is profound— does not spring from disease of thought— from moods of mind exalted at the expense of the general intellect. They who dream by day are cognizant of many things which escape those who dream only by night. In their gray visions they obtain glimpses of eternity, and thrill, in awakening, to find that they have been upon the verge of the great secret. In snatches, they "Eleonora" by Byam Shaw is in the public learn something of the wisdom which is of domain. good, and more of the mere knowledge which is of evil. They penetrate, however, rudderless or compassless into the vast ocean of the "light ineffable," and again, like the adventures of the Nubian geographer, "agressi sunt mare tenebrarum, quid in eo esset exploraturi." 1. Latin for “Under the protection of a specific form, the soul is safe.” 2. Ardor(noun)passion or enthusiasm 3. Cognizant(adjective)aware; having knowledge of 4. Lacking a clear sense of direction or purpose 5. Ineffable(adjective)too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words We will say, then, that I am mad. I grant, at least, that there are two distinct conditions of my mental existence— the condition of a lucid reason, not to be disputed, and belonging to the memory of events forming the first epoch of my life— and a condition of shadow and doubt, appertaining to the present, and to the recollection of what constitutes the second great era of my being. Therefore, what I shall tell of the earlier period, believe; and to what I may relate of the later time, give only such credit as may seem due, or doubt it altogether, or, if doubt it ye cannot, then play unto its riddle the Oedipus. She whom I loved in youth, and of whom I now pen calmly and distinctly these remembrances, was the sole daughter of the only sister of my mother long departed. Eleonora was the name of my cousin. We had always dwelled together, beneath a tropical sun, in the Valley of the Many- Colored Grass. No unguided footstep ever came upon that vale; for it lay away up among a range of giant hills that hung beetling around about it, shutting out the sunlight from its sweetest recesses. No path was trodden in its vicinity; and, to reach our happy home, there was need of putting back, with force, the foliage of many thousands of forest trees, and of crushing to death the glories of many millions of fragrant flowers. Thus it was that we lived all alone, knowing nothing of the world without the valley— I, and my cousin, and her mother. From the dim regions beyond the mountains at the upper end of our encircled domain, there crept out a narrow and deep river, brighter than all save the eyes of Eleonora; and, winding stealthily about in mazy courses, it passed away, at length, through a shadowy gorge, among hills still dimmer than those whence it had issued. We called it the "River of Silence"; for there seemed to be a hushing influence in its flow. No murmur arose from its bed, and so gently it wandered along, that the pearly pebbles upon which we loved to gaze, far down within its bosom, stirred not at all, but lay in a motionless content, each in its own old station, shining on gloriously forever. 12 13

The margin of the river, and of the many dazzling rivulets that glided through devious ways into its channel, as well as the spaces that extended from the margins away down into the depths of the streams until they reached the bed of pebbles at the bottom,—these spots, not less than the whole surface of the valley, from the river to the mountains that girdled it in, were carpeted all by a soft green grass, thick, short, perfectly even, and vanilla-perfumed, but so besprinkled throughout with the yellow buttercup, the white daisy, the purple violet, and the ruby-red asphodel, that its exceeding beauty spoke to our hearts in loud tones, of the love 6. Latin for “They ventured into the sea of darkness, to explore what it might contain” 7. Lucid(adjective)clear-thinking; unconfused 8. Oedipus, a character from Greek Mythology, solves a riddle whose answer is “man.” 9. Valley (poetically) 10. Overhanging, protruding 11. A steep, rocky valley 12. Small streams 13. In this context referring to a route, “devious” means longer and indirect and of the glory of God. And, here and there, in groves about this grass, like wildernesses of dreams, sprang up fantastic trees, whose tall slender stems stood not upright, but slanted gracefully toward the light that peered at noon-day into the centre of the valley. Their mark was speckled with the vivid alternate splendor of ebony and silver, and was smoother than all save the cheeks of Eleonora; so that, but for the brilliant green of the huge leaves that spread from their summits 15 16 in long, tremulous lines, dallying with the Zephyrs, one might have fancied them giant serpents of Syria doing homage to their sovereign the Sun. Hand in hand about this valley, for fifteen years, roamed I with Eleonora before Love entered 17 18 within our hearts. It was one evening at the close of the third lustrum of her life, and of the fourth of my own, that we sat, locked in each other's embrace, beneath the serpent-like trees, and looked down within the water of the River of Silence at our images therein. We spoke no words during the rest of that sweet day, and our words even upon the morrow were tremulous and few. We had drawn the God Eros from that wave, and now we felt that he had enkindled within us the fiery souls of our forefathers. The passions which had for centuries distinguished our race, came thronging with the fancies for which they had been equally noted, and together breathed a delirious bliss over the Valley of the Many-Colored Grass. A change fell upon all things. Strange, brilliant flowers, star-shaped, burn out upon the trees where no flowers had been known before. The tints of the green carpet deepened; and when, one by one, the white daisies shrank away, there sprang up in place of them, ten by ten of the ruby-red asphodel. And life arose in our paths; for the tall flamingo, hitherto unseen, with all gay glowing birds, flaunted his scarlet plumage before us. The golden and silver fish haunted the river, out of the bosom of which issued, little by little, a murmur that swelled, at length, into a lulling melody more divine than that of the harp of Aeolus —sweeter than all save the voice of Eleonora. And now, too, a voluminous cloud, which we had long watched in the regions of Hesper, floated out thence, all gorgeous in crimson and gold, and settling in peace above us, 14. Asphodel is a kind of flower related to the lily; in literature it is found in the Elysium Fields, Paradise in Greek Myths 15. Tremulous(adjective)shaking or quivery slightly; timid, nervous 16. A gentle breeze; Zephyr was also one of the four winds according to Greek Mythology. 17. In Poe’s time, romantic relationships with one’s first cousin were not considered as taboo as in today’s time. 18. A five-year period 19. The Greek God of love 20. Crowding or filling 21. Aeolus is the Greek God of the winds. An Aeolian harp is a musical instrument that can be played by the wind. 22. Voluminous(adjective)of great size, quantity, or volume 23. The western sky. According to Greek Mythology, the Hesperides were goddesses of the evening. In the Garden of the Hesperides, there was a tree whose golden apples could grant sank, day by day, lower and lower, until its edges rested upon the tops of the mountains, turning all their dimness into magnificence, and shutting us up, as if forever, within a magic prison-house of grandeur and of glory. The loveliness of Eleonora was that of the Seraphim; but she was a maiden artless and 25 26 innocent as the brief life she had led among the flowers. No guile disguised the fervor of love which animated her heart, and she examined with me its inmost recesses as we walked together in the Valley of the Many-Colored Grass, and discoursed of the mighty changes which had lately taken place therein. At length, having spoken one day, in tears, of the last sad change which must befall Humanity, she thenceforward dwelt only upon this one sorrowful theme, interweaving it into all our 27 28 converse, as, in the songs of the bard of Schiraz, the same images are found occurring, again and again, in every impressive variation of phrase.

She had seen that the finger of Death was upon her bosom— that, like the ephemeron, she had been made perfect in loveliness only to die; but the terrors of the grave to her lay solely in a consideration which she revealed to me, one evening at twilight, by the banks of the River of Silence. She grieved to think that, having entombed her in the Valley of the Many-Colored Grass, I would quit forever its happy recesses, transferring the love which now was so passionately her own to some maiden of the outer and everyday world. And, then and there, I threw myself hurriedly at the feet of Eleonora, and offered up a vow, to herself and to Heaven, that I would never bind myself in marriage to any daughter of Earth— that I would in no manner prove recreant to her dear memory, or to the memory of the devout affection with which she had blessed me. And I called the Mighty Ruler of the Universe to witness the pious solemnity of my vow. And the curse which I invoked of Him and of her, a saint in Helusion should I prove traitorous to that promise, involved a penalty the exceeding great horror of which will not permit me to make record of it here. And the bright eyes of Eleonora grew brighter at my words; and she sighed as if a deadly burthen had been taken from her breast; and she trembled and very bitterly wept; but she made acceptance of the vow, (for what was immortality. 24. (Singular: seraph) Angelic beings, like Cherubs, from a Christian tradition, associated with ardor, light, and purity. 25. Sly intelligence 26. Fervor(noun)intense passion 27. Conversations 28. A Sufi poet who was known to praise women in his writing 29. An insect that lives for only one day 30. Unfaithful 31. Pious(adjective)devoutly religious 32. Paradise (also spelled “Elysion” or “Elysium”) 33. Archaic spelling of "burden" she but a child?) and it made easy to her the bed of her death. And she said to me, not many days afterward, tranquilly dying, that, because of what I had done for the comfort of her spirit she would watch over me in that spirit when departed, and, if so it were permitted her return to me visibly in the watches of the night; but, if this thing were, indeed, beyond the power of the souls in Paradise, that she would, at least, give me frequent indications of her presence, sighing upon me in the evening winds, or filling the air which I breathed with perfume from the censers of the angels. And, with these words upon her lips, she yielded up her innocent life, putting an end to the first epoch of my own. Thus far I have faithfully said. But as I pass the barrier in Times path, formed by the death of my beloved, and proceed with the second era of my existence, I feel that a shadow gathers over my brain, and I mistrust the perfect sanity of the record. But let me on.— Years dragged themselves along heavily, and still I dwelled within the Valley of the Many-Colored Grass; but a second change had come upon all things. The star-shaped flowers shrank into the stems of the trees, and appeared no more. The tints of the green carpet faded; and, one by one, the ruby- red asphodels withered away; and there sprang up, in place of them, ten by ten, dark, eye-like violets, that writhed uneasily and were ever encumbered with dew. And Life departed from our paths; for the tall flamingo flaunted no longer his scarlet plumage before us, but flew sadly from the vale into the hills, with all the gay glowing birds that had arrived in his company. And the golden and silver fish swam down through the gorge at the lower end of our domain and bedecked the sweet river never again. And the lulling melody that had been softer than the wind-harp of Aeolus, and more divine than all save the voice of Eleonora, it died little by little away, in murmurs growing lower and lower, until the stream returned, at length, utterly, into the solemnity of its original silence. And then, lastly, the voluminous cloud uprose, and, abandoning the tops of the mountains to the dimness of old, fell back into the regions of Hesper, and took away all its manifold golden and gorgeous glories from the Valley of the Many-Colored Grass. Yet the promises of Eleonora were not forgotten; for I heard the sounds of the swinging of the censers of the angels; and streams of a holy perfume floated ever and ever about the valley; and at lone hours, when my heart beat heavily, the winds that bathed my brow came unto me laden with soft sighs; and indistinct murmurs filled often the night air, and once— oh, but once only! I was awakened from a slumber, like the slumber of death, by the pressing of spiritual lips upon my own. But the void within my heart refused, even thus, to be filled. I longed for the love which had before filled it to overflowing. At length the valley pained me through its memories of Eleonora, and I left it for ever for the vanities and the turbulent triumphs of the world. 34. Tranquilly(adverb)calmly 35. A container used to burn incense 36. Epoch(noun)a period of time I found myself within a strange city, where all things might have served to blot from recollection the sweet dreams I had dreamed so long in the Valley of the Many-Colored Grass. The pomps and pageantries of a stately court, and the mad clangor of arms, and the radiant loveliness of women, bewildered and intoxicated my brain. But as yet my soul had proved true to its vows, and the indications of the presence of Eleonora were still given me in the silent hours of the night. Suddenly these manifestations they ceased, and the world grew dark before mine eyes, and I stood aghast at the burning thoughts which possessed, at the terrible temptations which beset me; for there came from some far, far distant and unknown land, into the gay court of the king I served, a maiden to whose beauty my whole recreant heart yielded at once— at whose footstool I bowed down without a struggle, in the most ardent, in the most abject worship of love. What, indeed, was my passion for the young girl of the valley in comparison with the fervor, and the delirium, and the spirit-lifting ecstasy of adoration with which I poured 39 40 out my whole soul in tears at the feet of the ethereal Ermengarde? — Oh, bright was the seraph Ermengarde! and in that knowledge I had room for none other.— Oh, divine was the angel Ermengarde! and as I looked down into the depths of her memorial eyes, I thought only of them— and of her.

I wedded;— nor dreaded the curse I had invoked; and its bitterness was not visited upon me. And once— but once again in the silence of the night; there came through my lattice the soft sighs which had forsaken me; and they modelled themselves into familiar and sweet voice, saying: "Sleep in peace!— for the Spirit of Love reigneth and ruleth, and, in taking to thy passionate heart her who is Ermengarde, thou art absolved, for reasons which shall be made known to thee in Heaven, of thy vows unto Eleonora."

articles.title: The Cooking Lesson
articles.author: Sasha Hom
articles.description: A daughter struggles with the expectations of her Chinese American parents.
articles.intro: Sasha Hom was adopted from South Korea in 1975 and grew up in Berkeley, California in a Chinese American family. In this short story about an Asian-American family, the narrator prepares dinner for her family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify evidence that demonstrates the significance of the chimichanga.
articles.permissions: "The Cooking Lesson" by Sasha Hom. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: All I want is a chimichanga from the freezer. But today is Friday, my day to cook. I stand at the counter chopping vegetables the way Ma taught me — at a diagonal with the tip of the cleaver always touching the board. My little sister watches sitcoms on the one TV that has more than three channels, placed conveniently for her on the kitchen table. She laughs loudly at the show. “It’s just a movement of the wrist,” Ma says, standing behind me. “I know. That’s what I’m doing,” I say, wondering how I’m ever going to get away tonight. I look out the window. "Person Chopping Carrot" by Nothing Ahead is “No, you’re not,” Ma says. “Try harder.” licensed under CC0.

Ma grew up in a Chinese restaurant. Her father was the chef. He had immigrated from China with a paper name only to get stuck on Angel Island for weeks, along with others in the same boat, so to speak. He said the immigration officials would drill each family member asking questions like, “How many boards made up your floor? How many rafters in your ceiling?” If they had differing answers, they’d have to stay even longer. Some people just gave up, realizing that they’d come all this way only to get stuck on an island smaller than the province they left. There are still poems written on the walls by the detainees. 1. a deep fried burrito that is common in Southwestern U.S. cuisine Ma walks over to my little sister and stands behind her at the table. She starts watching Silver Spoons alongside my sister while brushing her scraggly hair into a ponytail. “Ouch. You’re hurting me,” my sister protests. Ma says something to herself in Chinese.

Ma’s dad taught himself how to speak English, write, and cook. He made out better than Dad’s parents, who Dad says died poor. Ma’s dad became the first Chinese American in Oakland, California to get a liquor license. Ma was just the hostess. But she considers herself a “foodie.” “You’re moving your whole shoulder. Pay closer attention. Each cut has to be precise, for the flavor.” “Oh, great,” I say, more to myself, glancing wistfully out the window. I get on my tippy toes to get a better angle at the board. Ma’s always complaining about how everything is made for “Americans” — counters, sinks, cars, jeans, even toilets — not taking into consideration the shorter races, I suppose. I have to concentrate real hard to make that nice rhythmic sound that she makes. Chop-chop-chop-chop-chop. My rhythm always comes out stilted. Chop… chop, chop… chop… clatter. “Dad should be home soon,” Ma says, meaning, hurry up, do it right and make the freaking dinner, already.

Dad’s never “home soon.” Usually, I don’t hear the garage door open until I’m already upstairs fighting with Algebra and gravity — that is numbers that won’t add and eyelids that won’t stay open. How is it that schools can give us six hours of homework a night, hold us hostage for eight-hours a day when we live an hour away, with the doctors saying we need nine-and-a-half hours of sleep, and still expect us to stay awake during class? Somebody needs to add the numbers again to make sure the total sum is equal to, or less than, twenty-four. But if I complain about anything, my parents turn it around and use it as a way to show me my shortcomings. “Just be patient,” they say, when I’m told I’m too young to date. “Be humble,” they say, when they march me back up the stairs to change out of my skirt. I don’t need to be shown my shortcomings. All I have to do is look in a mirror.

“And for crying out loud, can’t you just be grateful?!” they say if I ever complain about Dad not being home to help me with my math, which I’m eternally flunking (so much for the stereotype that Asians are good at math). “He has to work so hard for us. You know? Because of the glass ceiling,” Ma says as if it’s his boss. Then she’ll recite all the sacrifices they’ve made for us like it’s the Constitution, my one- way ticket to the American Dream. If it’s anything like the game Monopoly, then I keep picking the wrong Chance card. Go Directly to Jail. Do Not Pass Go. Do Not Collect $200, or whatever it is. The phone rings. I try to wipe the food off my hand on the dirty dish towel to answer, but Ma beats me to it. “Hello? Who is this? I’m sorry. She’s not here right now. Can I take a message?” she says, hanging up so quickly that the person on the other line couldn’t possibly have had time to give any sort of message. “Who was that?” I ask, wondering if it was Roland, my boyfriend, who is two years older than me and not Chinese.

If asked what he is, he’ll say, “Oakland.” If pressed to just check a dumb box, he might check, “Black/African American,” but then turn around and say, “Sure. But most of my ‘Africa’ comes out of Oakland,” as if his people were formed from the clay of Lake Merritt. For me, I just check, “Asian.” Easy-peasy, lemon-squeezy, as they say — just one small box for another expansive continent. Ma is suspiciously silent. “Who was that?” I repeat. “Nobody,” she says, glaring at me.

But then, outside the kitchen window, I see Roland standing there with his cell phone to his ear like he’s still trying to leave a message with Ma. My neighbor, Mr. Neil, is in his rocking chair on the front porch. His hairy white belly hangs out of his American flag shirt, resting like a mini poodle on his lap. Mr. Neil is alright, most times but… whenever he’s dropping a dime into my Unicef box, or ordering a box of Girl Scout Cookies from me, he always has something to say to me about “red” China and asks if my family ever considers moving back. He smokes his cigarette and eyes Roland, and I know he won’t leave the porch until Roland is gone. A cop car passes by. Roland spots me and waves enthusiastically, his face as wide and open as an ocean, and in that moment, I realize that I love him, that he’s the purest heart I will ever know. I put down the cleaver, wanting to go out and see him. I wave and tentatively hold up a finger as if to say, I’ll be there in a moment, which we both know might just be my hopes talking. My stomach growls and without thinking, I open the freezer and pull out that chimichanga that I can’t seem to take my mind off — shredded steak and cheese, sold in an 18-pack from Costco. I pop open the bag and slip it into the microwave, punching the exact sequence of numbers necessary before hitting, “Start.” I hear the garage door as it begins to peel off the concrete. I look at the clock. Dad is early. Through the window, I watch as his Kia turns the corner up the driveway. I picture him slouched over the wheel, clutching it tightly, passing by Roland — perhaps oblivious, perhaps not. I hear him as he parks the car, taking some time to get all that he needs from out of it. I can see him now through the screen door that leads to the kitchen. He checks to make sure he’s locked all the doors, even though it’s parked inside the garage, then rubs some invisible blemish off the window before pushing open the kitchen door. He pauses at the threshold and takes a deep breath in, his tired lined face breaking into a big slow smile.

“I’m home,” he says, like he’s accomplished something. My sister looks up briefly from the screen. She does not say hi. “Hey, Dad,” I say, enthusiastically, for both of us. I force a big cheery smile. I look out the window but I don’t see Roland. “I’m making dinner tonight. Why don’t you take a seat?” He sets his stuff in the corner. I sigh. I pick up the cleaver and start chopping, focusing on the tip of my cleaver as if my life depends on it. I pause to look out the window and, sure enough, Roland is gone. My neighbor’s empty chair is still rocking. Then, I resume: Chop — chop — chop — chop. The microwave beeps but I don’t stop to open its door. Dad does, though. He pulls out the chimichanga with his fingertips, unrolls the wrapper, and inhales it, steaming hot, in less than a minute flat, smiling at me with bits of beef in his teeth.
“Thanks,” he says. “Any time,” I answer.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Describe a time when you felt like what you wanted for yourself and what your family wanted were different. What did you do?
What are some ways that the story conveys the unique experience of being the daughter of Chinese immigrants? What are some themes of the story that are universal?
At the end of the story, the narrator says, "Any time" after her father eats her chimichanga. How do you think she was feeling at that moment? What would you have said if you were in her position?
